question: What is the per-kilometer commuting deduction rate for travel by one's own car (oma auto) in 2025, and how does that compare to the rate for a moped?
answer_generated: The per-kilometer commuting deduction rate for travel by one's own car (oma auto) in 2025 is 0,27 €/km. The rate for a moped is 0,13 €/km. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Verohallinnon yhtenäistämisohjeet vuodelta 2025 toimitettavaa verotusta varten\Verohallinnon yhtenäistämisohjeet vuodelta 2025 toimitettavaa verotusta varten - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: For the 2025 tax return, the commuting deduction (asunnon ja työpaikan väliset matkat) rate for travel by one's own car (oma auto) is 0.27 euros per kilometre, while the rate for a moped (mopo) is 0.13 euros per kilometre. The own-car rate is therefore more than twice the moped rate (0.27 €/km vs. 0.13 €/km — a difference of 0.14 €/km). The same per-kilometre rates also apply to occasional work trips (työmatkat). Commuting deductions for the home–workplace journey are normally calculated according to the cheapest available means of transport; own-car or moped rates apply only when their use is justified.

question: What withholding tax rate (lähdevero-%) applies to a foreign specialist who has been officially granted key personnel (avainhenkilö) tax status in Finland, and what is the maximum number of months their tax card remains valid from the start of employment?
answer_generated: The withholding tax rate (lähdevero-%) for a foreign specialist officially granted key personnel (avainhenkilö) tax status in Finland is 32%. The maximum number of months their tax card remains valid from the start of employment is 48 months. (Source: Avainhenkilöltä perittävä lähdevero vuodesta 2020 alkaen - vero.fi)
answer_expected: Under the key-personnel act (avainhenkilölaki, 1551/1995), a foreign specialist who has been granted key-personnel tax status pays a flat withholding tax (lähdevero) on their Finnish salary instead of progressive income tax. For wages paid up to 31 December 2025 the rate is 32%, and for wages paid on or after 1 January 2026 the rate has been lowered to 25%. The key-personnel tax card is granted for the period of work in Finland reported in the application, but its validity is capped: under the rules in force from 2020 it could be valid for at most 48 months from the start of employment, and from 1 January 2026 onwards the maximum has been extended to 84 months from the original start of employment (an extension can be applied for in writing within 30 days of the previous card's expiry). The application must be filed in writing with the Tax Administration before the work starts in Finland or within 90 days of starting.

question: What is the gift tax (lahjavero) threshold below which a recipient owes no gift tax, and how are gifts from the same donor within the same three-year window treated when computing that threshold?
answer_generated: The gift tax (lahjavero) threshold below which a recipient owes no gift tax is 3,400 euros. Gifts from the same donor within the same three-year window are aggregated to determine if the threshold is exceeded. If the total value of these gifts exceeds 3,400 euros, gift tax is due on the aggregate amount. (PerVL 19 §)
answer_expected: Under the Finnish Inheritance and Gift Tax Act (perintö- ja lahjaverolaki, PerVL 18 §), as updated effective 1 January 2026, a gift recipient (lahjansaaja) is liable for gift tax (lahjavero) only if the value of the gift is at least 7,500 euros. Gifts received from the same donor within a three-year period are aggregated when computing the 7,500 euro threshold — i.e., all gifts from the same giver during any rolling three-year window are added together, and gift tax becomes payable once the cumulative amount reaches 7,500 euros. If the threshold is exceeded, the recipient must file a gift tax return (lahjaveroilmoitus) with the Finnish Tax Administration within three months from the date the recipient took possession of the gift.

question: What is the maximum daily withholding tax percentage (ennakonpidätys) that can be applied to a taxpayer's income under Finnish law?
answer_generated: The maximum possible withholding tax percentage (ennakonpidätys) that can be applied to a taxpayer's income under Finnish law is 60.0%. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Päätökset\Verohallinnon päätös ennakonpidätysprosenttien laskentaperusteista palkkatuloa varten ja ennakonkannossa määrättävän ennakkoveron laskentaperusteista vuodelle 2026\Verohallinnon päätös ennakonpidätysprosenttien laskentaperusteista palkkatuloa varten ja ennakonkannossa määrättävän ennakkoveron laskentaperusteista vuodelle 2026 - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: Under the Finnish Tax Administration's decision on the calculation bases for withholding tax percentages on wage income for 2026 (Verohallinnon päätös ennakonpidätysprosenttien laskentaperusteista palkkatuloa varten ... vuodelle 2026), the withholding tax percentage (ennakonpidätysprosentti) is confirmed in increments of half a percentage point and rounded upwards. It can be at most 60.0 percent. The supplementary percentage (lisäprosentti, applied to income above the personal income limit) is calculated to half a percentage point precision, must be at least two percentage points higher than the base withholding percentage, and likewise has a maximum of 60.0 percent. The decision applies to payments made on or after 1 January 2026.

question: What is the Finnish broadcasting tax (yleisradiovero) rate in 2026, what is the annual income floor below which it is not levied, and what is the annual maximum amount?
answer_generated: The Finnish broadcasting tax (yleisradiovero) rate in 2026 is 2.5%. The annual income floor below which it is not levied is 15,150 euros. The annual maximum amount is 160 euros. (Source: YleveroL 2 §)
answer_expected: Under the 2026 Verohallinto withholding decision (section 7.2.1), the personal broadcasting tax (yleisradiovero) for 2026 is calculated as 2.5 percent of the portion of the taxpayer's combined net earned income and capital income (or, if higher, the combined YEL and/or MYEL pension-insurance work income) that exceeds 15,150 euros per year. Below that 15,150 euro threshold no broadcasting tax is owed. The maximum annual amount of yleisradiovero is 160 euros. The tax applies to persons who are generally tax-resident in Finland and who have reached the age of 18 by the end of the tax year at the latest, and is also still levied for the year of death. It is not levied on persons who were limitedly tax-resident for the whole year, nor on natural persons or estates whose home municipality is in the Åland province (those persons pay instead the Åland media fee, 128 euros). No tax-from-tax deductions reduce the broadcasting tax.

question: What is the statutory pension insurance contribution rate (TyEL-maksu) deducted from an employee's wages in 2026, and up to what upper age limit does the insurance obligation continue for employees born in 1962 or later?
answer_generated: The context does not provide the specific statutory pension insurance contribution rate (TyEL-maksu) for 2026. However, it indicates that the rates can vary by year and are set annually by the Ministry of Social Affairs and Health. For the upper age limit, the insurance obligation continues until the age of 68, 69, or 70, depending on the employee's birth year. For employees born in 1962 or later, the upper age limit is 68 years (TyEL 4 §:n 2 momentti). Source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Kotitalous työn teettäjänä\Kotitalous työn teettäjänä - vero.fi.html.
answer_expected: According to the Finnish Tax Administration's 2026 withholding decision (section 4.3.1), the employee pension insurance contribution (eläkevakuutusmaksu / TyEL employee share) used as a deduction from withholding-taxable wages and seafarer income is 7.30 percent of the gross pay. The contribution starts to accrue from the beginning of the calendar month following the month in which the employee turns 17, and continues until the upper age limit of the insurance obligation is reached. That upper age limit is staggered by birth cohort: 68 years for persons born in 1957 or earlier, 69 years for persons born 1958-1961, and 70 years for persons born in 1962 or later. So for an employee born in 1962 or later, the TyEL employee contribution continues to be deducted up until age 70.

question: A popular Finnish blogger receives free beauty products worth approximately 150 euros per month from a cosmetics company in exchange for reviews. The company also occasionally gives her branded tote bags with its logo worth about 5 euros each. Are either of these items taxable income? Does it matter whether the blogger transfers copyright over the blog post to the company?
answer_generated:
The beauty products worth approximately 150 euros per month and the branded tote bags worth about 5 euros each are considered taxable income for the blogger. According to the guidelines, any compensation received in exchange for blog posts, including non-monetary benefits, is taxable. The value of these items should be included in the blogger's ansiotulo (earned income).

If the blogger transfers the copyright over the blog post to the company, the compensation received, including the value of the beauty products and tote bags, is classified as käyttökorvaus (usage fee) under Ennakkoperintälain 25 §:n 1 momentin 2 kohdassa. This means that the value of the items is still taxable as ansiotulo.

In summary, both the beauty products and the tote bags are taxable income, and the transfer of copyright does not change this classification. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Blogikirjoituksista saatujen korvausten ja etujen verotus\Blogikirjoituksista saatujen korvausten ja etujen verotus - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: Beauty products (~150 euros/month): These are taxable earned income (ansiotulo) for the blogger. Vero's kannanotto on bloggers expressly states that a blogger's taxable earned income includes, alongside cash compensation, 'erilaiset tavarat ja etuudet, alennukset, lahjakortit, matkat sekä tapahtumakutsut' (various goods and benefits, discounts, gift cards, trips and event invitations) received in connection with blogging. Non-cash compensation is valued at fair market value (kayepa arvo). Because the products are given in exchange for reviews, they are compensation for work and qualify as taxable income — typically as either palkka (wages), tyokorvaus (work compensation under EPL 25 sec 1 para 1) or, where the activity meets business criteria, business income.  Branded tote bags (~5 euros each): These are NOT taxable income because they qualify as 'tavanomaiset mainoslahjat' (ordinary advertising gifts) — items of low value bearing the advertiser's logo that cannot easily be converted into cash. Vero specifically excludes these from taxable income. EXCEPTION: if such a logo item has been agreed as the actual compensation for a specific blog post, then its fair market value is taxable.  Does copyright transfer matter? Yes — it changes the legal characterisation of the compensation but not whether it is taxable. If the blogger keeps copyright, the beauty products are tyokorvaus under EPL 25 sec 1 para 1 (work compensation) or palkka if the relationship has employment characteristics. If the blogger transfers copyright over the blog post to the company (e.g. assigns the post for the company's use), the same compensation is reclassified as kayttokorvaus (royalty/use compensation under EPL 25 sec 1 para 2). Both are taxable earned income; the distinction affects withholding category and how the payer must report and withhold tax, but not the taxability itself. Note: Vero withdrew this kannanotto on 29.11.2021 and now treats blogger income under the broader 'Tulot blogista, vlogista ja somekanavista' guidance — but the substantive principles (fair-market valuation of in-kind compensation, the mainoslahja exception, and the work-compensation vs use-compensation distinction) remain the framework.

question: A company purchases electronic meal vouchers through a third-party voucher platform (app) for its employees. The platform invoices the company for the vouchers but does not charge VAT on the voucher face value. Can the company claim a VAT deduction on the invoice? How does the answer change if the company instead contracts directly with a restaurant for catering services?
answer_generated:
The company cannot claim a VAT deduction on the invoice for the electronic meal vouchers purchased through a third-party voucher platform. According to the provided context, the third-party platform (A) does not charge VAT on the face value of the lounassetele (meal vouchers) when they are sold to companies (B and C). Therefore, since no VAT is charged, there is no VAT to be deducted (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Arvoseteleiden arvonlisäverotuksesta\Arvoseteleiden arvonlisäverotuksesta - vero.fi.html).

If the company instead contracts directly with a restaurant for catering services, the situation changes. In this case, the restaurant would charge VAT on the services provided. The company can then claim a VAT deduction on the invoice for the catering services, provided that the services are used in the company's taxable activities (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Henkilöstöruokailun arvonlisäverotuksesta\Henkilöstöruokailun arvonlisäverotuksesta - vero.fi.html).
answer_expected: Electronic meal vouchers via a third-party platform — no VAT deduction on the voucher value: Per KHO 2025:46 (as incorporated into the Vero guidance 'Henkilostoruokailun arvonlisaverotuksesta'), an electronic application-based meal benefit administered by a platform operator (B Oy) is treated for VAT purposes as a multi-purpose voucher (arvoseteli) equivalent to a paper lunch voucher. KHO held that 'arvosetelin hankkimista ja luovutusta ei ollut pidettava hyodykkeen ostamisena ja myymisena' — the acquisition and transfer of the voucher is NOT a supply of goods/services for VAT purposes. The actual restaurant/catering service is supplied by the restaurant directly to the employee, not to the employer. Therefore, even if the platform operator issues an invoice in the restaurant's name and account showing VAT, the employer has no right to deduct that VAT, because the employer is not the recipient of the catering service. Vero's guidance summarises the case: 'tyonantajan ostama lounasseteli ei sisaltanyt vahennettavaa arvonlisaveroa eika tyonantaja suorittanut arvonlisaveroa tyontekijalleen luovuttamasta lounassetelista.'  However, the SEPARATE platform administration/handling fee (the service fee charged by the platform for managing the voucher scheme, distinct from the voucher face value) IS deductible as a general business overhead (yleiskulu). KHO 2025:46 explicitly stated this and the Vero guidance confirms: 'lounasseteleiden hallinnointiin liittyvan palvelun veloitus on tyonantajalle vahennyskelpoinen yleiskulu' — subject to AVL 117 sec apportionment if the employer has both taxable and exempt activity.  Direct contract with a restaurant for catering — VAT IS deductible: When the employer contracts directly with a restaurant for catering and the restaurant invoices the employer with VAT (13.5% rate on restaurant/meal services), the employer's situation differs: the employer is the recipient of the catering supply. The employer must then either (a) account for self-supply VAT (oman kayton vero) under AVL 25 a sec if meals are given to employees free of charge — but can deduct the input VAT charged by the restaurant; or (b) sell the meals onward to employees, charging VAT on the consideration received (with possible markup to fair market value under the related-party rules of AVL 73 c–73 e sec if the price is below the ravintoetu raha-arvo). In both cases the input VAT on the restaurant's invoice is deductible because there is a real supply of catering to the employer. This is also confirmed by KHO 2001:19, which treated additional employer subsidies paid to a contract restaurant as deductible general overheads.

question: A Finnish resident wins 10,000 euros in a licensed lottery in Germany. Another Finnish resident wins the same amount from a licensed lottery in Canada (outside the ETA area). How is each win taxed in Finland — is it tax-free or taxable, and if taxable, under which income category?
answer_generated:
The Finnish resident who wins 10,000 euros in a licensed lottery in Germany (an EU/ETA country) does not have to pay tax on this win in Finland. According to TVL 85 §, a win from a lottery conducted in another EU/ETA country in accordance with that country's laws is tax-free for the recipient (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Lotto- ja muiden arpajaisvoittojen jakaminen usean henkilön kesken\Lotto- ja muiden arpajaisvoittojen jakaminen usean henkilön kesken - vero.fi.html).

The Finnish resident who wins 10,000 euros in a licensed lottery in Canada (outside the EU/ETA area) must pay tax on this win in Finland. The win is considered other taxable earned income (muu ansiotulo) according to TVL 29 §:n 1 momentti and 61 §:n 1 momentti (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Muualla kuin Suomessa tai EU_ETA-alueella järjestetyistä vedonlyönti, veikkaus- ja raha-automaattipeleistä saatavien tulojen verotus tuloverolain mukaan\Muualla kuin Suomessa tai EU_ETA-alueella järjestetyistä vedonlyönti, veikkaus- ja raha-automaattipeleistä saatavien tulojen verotus tuloverolain mukaan - vero.fi.html).
answer_expected: Germany (within EEA / ETA-alue): TAX-FREE in Finland. Under TVL 85 sec, lottery winnings from a lottery operated under the legislation of a state belonging to the European Economic Area (Euroopan talousalue, ETA) are not taxable income. Germany is an EU/EEA member state, so a 10,000 EUR win from a licensed German lottery is verovapaa arpajaisvoitto for the Finnish-resident winner — provided the winner is the person who, under that country's legislation, participated as the identified player and to whom the operator paid the winnings. No Finnish income tax is due, and no income category applies because the win is exempt.  Canada (outside EEA): TAXABLE in Finland as 'muuta veronalaista ansiotuloa' — other taxable earned income. The Vero kannanotto explicitly states: 'Euroopan talousalueen (ETA-alueen) ulkopuolelta saadut arpajaisvoitot ovat saajalleen muuta veronalaista ansiotuloa' (lottery winnings from outside the EEA are other taxable earned income for the recipient). Canada is outside the EEA, so the full 10,000 EUR win is included in the Finnish resident's earned income (ansiotulo) and taxed at progressive earned-income rates. The principle applies regardless of whether the foreign lottery is otherwise licensed/regulated in its home country — the EEA boundary is what matters under TVL 85 sec.

question: Five colleagues agree informally to pool their money to buy lottery tickets. Only one colleague (Matti) registers the ticket under his name and wins 500,000 euros. Matti distributes 100,000 euros to each of the other four colleagues based on their pre-draw agreement. (a) What is the tax treatment for Matti's own 100,000-euro share? (b) What is the tax treatment for each of the other four colleagues' 100,000-euro shares? (c) Does the gift tax threshold affect any of these payments?
answer_generated:
(a) For Matti's own 100,000-euro share, the entire amount is considered taxable income as a lottery win. According to Finnish tax law, lottery winnings are classified as other income and are fully taxable.

(b) Each of the other four colleagues' 100,000-euro shares is also considered taxable income as a lottery win. Similar to Matti, the full amount received by each colleague is classified as other income and is fully taxable.

(c) The gift tax threshold does not apply to these payments because they are considered lottery winnings, which are taxable as income, not as gifts. Therefore, the gift tax threshold is irrelevant in this context. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Lotto- ja muiden arpajaisvoittojen jakaminen usean henkilön kesken\Lotto- ja muiden arpajaisvoittojen jakaminen usean henkilön kesken - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: Background: Veikkaus Oy must register and identify each player under arpajaislaki 14 sec. Only winnings paid to a registered/identified player are TVL 85 sec verovapaa arpajaisvoitto. Vero's kannanotto specifically addresses an informal pooling arrangement where only one person buys/registers the ticket and an informal pre-draw agreement exists to share winnings. The case described matches that fact pattern.  (a) Matti's own 100,000 EUR share: TAX-FREE under TVL 85 sec. Matti is the sole registered/identified player under arpajaislaki 14 sec, so he alone is treated as the lottery-winning recipient. His own 100,000 EUR share is verovapaa arpajaisvoitto. Note: the entire 500,000 EUR is, in the first instance, his tax-free lottery winning under TVL 85 sec.  (b) The other four colleagues' 100,000 EUR shares each: TAXABLE as 'muuta veronalaista ansiotuloa' (other taxable earned income). Vero's kannanotto is explicit: 'Jos voiton lunastanut henkilo jakaa yksin saamansa arpajaisvoiton toisten henkilöiden kanssa ennen arvontaa tehdyn yhdessa pelaamista koskevan sopimuksen perusteella, arpajaisvoitto on verovapaa vain tunnistautuneelle ja voiton lunastaneelle pelaajalle ja muiden sopimuksen osapuolten saama tulo katsotaan muuksi veronalaiseksi ansiotuloksi.' Each of the four colleagues includes 100,000 EUR in their earned income and is taxed at progressive earned-income rates. This is NOT treated as a verovapaa lottery share and NOT as a gift, because the distribution is based on a pre-draw informal pooling agreement.  (c) Gift tax threshold: NOT directly applicable to these four 100,000 EUR payments. Because the kannanotto reclassifies the four colleagues' shares as taxable earned income (not lahja under PerVL 18 sec), the gift tax threshold has no role for these specific payments. The distinction is critical: gift treatment applies only when the lottery winner gives away the winnings AFTER the draw without a pre-existing pooling agreement. The 7,500 EUR threshold (in force from 1.1.2026; previously 5,000 EUR) is the floor below which the lahjaverotus does not apply, and same-donor gifts within three years are aggregated. So if Matti had instead voluntarily 'gifted' colleagues 100,000 EUR each AFTER his win without any pre-draw agreement, each gift would be a PerVL 18 sec lahja, well above the 7,500 EUR threshold, and each colleague would owe lahjavero (and must file a lahjaveroilmoitus within three months). In the question's facts (pre-draw agreement), the threshold does not affect the outcome — the four shares are simply taxable earned income.

question: An employee works in a hospital canteen. The employer's direct cost per meal is 5.00 euros. Using the standard indirect cost ratio recognized in Finnish tax guidance, what total cost per meal should be used to determine the taxable meal benefit (ravintoetu), and how does this compare to the institutional catering threshold for supervised meals (e.g., patients)?
answer_generated:
The standard indirect cost ratio recognized in Finnish tax guidance is 22%. Therefore, the indirect costs for the meal would be calculated as follows: 22% of 5.00 euros = 1.10 euros. Adding the direct costs and indirect costs together, the total cost per meal is 5.00 euros + 1.10 euros = 6.10 euros.

For institutional catering in hospitals, the threshold for supervised meals (e.g., patients) is 5.28 euros per meal, as per Verohallinnon päätös 12 §. 

Thus, the total cost per meal of 6.10 euros exceeds the institutional catering threshold of 5.28 euros for supervised meals. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Henkilöstöruokailun arvonlisäverotuksesta\Henkilöstöruokailun arvonlisäverotuksesta - vero.fi.html, data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Päätökset\Verohallinnon päätös vuodelta 2026 toimitettavassa verotuksessa noudatettavista luontoisetujen laskentaperusteista\Verohallinnon päätös vuodelta 2026 toimitettavassa verotuksessa noudatettavista luontoisetujen laskentaperusteista - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: Standard indirect-cost ratio: 22% of direct costs. Per Vero's guidance 'Henkilostoruokailun arvonlisaverotuksesta' (section 3, drawing on AVL 75 sec and the related Verohallinto guidance on oman kayton verotus), 'Verovelvollinen voi halutessaan kayttaa valillisten kustannusten maarana 22 prosenttia valittomista kustannuksista' — the taxpayer may use 22% of direct costs as the indirect costs.  Total cost per meal calculation: - Direct costs (valittomat kustannukset): 5.00 EUR - Indirect costs (valilliset kustannukset) at 22%: 0.22 x 5.00 = 1.10 EUR - TOTAL cost per meal: 5.00 + 1.10 = 6.10 EUR  Comparison to institutional catering thresholds (laitosruokailu) for 2026: - Standard ravintoetu raha-arvo: 8.80 EUR/meal (applies if direct costs incl. VAT are 8.80–14.00 EUR; outside that band, the value equals the direct costs incl. VAT). - Hospital/school/daycare/similar institution staff (laitosruokailu): 6.60 EUR/meal. - Same staff when the meal occurs in the SUPERVISION of pupils' or patients' meals (oppilaiden tai hoidettavien ruokailun valvonnan yhteydessa): 5.28 EUR/meal. - Hotel/restaurant staff and flight crew during a flight: 7.48 EUR/meal.  So for the hospital canteen employee: - Computed total cost per meal: 6.10 EUR - Standard institutional catering threshold for hospital staff: 6.60 EUR - Supervised-meals threshold (eating with patients): 5.28 EUR  Result: 6.10 EUR sits BELOW the standard institutional 6.60 EUR threshold and ABOVE the supervised-meals 5.28 EUR threshold. The taxable ravintoetu raha-arvo for ordinary hospital staff in 2026 would in principle be 6.60 EUR (since the standard institutional figure governs unless the meal is taken in supervision of patients). If the meal is eaten in supervision of patients, the value drops to 5.28 EUR. If the employer charges the employee a price below the applicable raha-arvo, AVL 73 c–73 e sec alivastikkeellinen rules can require correcting the VAT base to fair market value.  Note on caveat: The raha-arvo thresholds in luontoisetu decision (10 sec) typically reference direct costs INCLUDING VAT for the 8.80–14.00 EUR band; the question gives a 5.00 EUR figure without specifying inclusion of VAT. The 22% indirect-cost markup is the standard option from oman kayton verotus VAT guidance and is reused in Vero's institutional-meal raha-arvo determination.

question: An employee has a company-provided benefit car (käyttöetuauto) available for private use. They also own their own car. For commuting (asunto–työpaikka matkat), what per-kilometer deduction rate applies when they drive their own car, versus when they drive the company benefit car? Which vehicle does Finnish tax practice assume they should use for the deduction?
answer_generated:
When an employee drives their own car for commuting, the per-kilometer deduction rate is higher compared to when they drive a company-provided benefit car. Specifically, the deduction rate for driving a company-provided benefit car is 0.20 euros per kilometer (PerVL 1 § + 2 §). The exact rate for the employee's own car is not specified in the provided context, but it is noted that the rate for a company-provided benefit car is lower than for the employee's own car (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Luontoisetuauton vaikutus kilometrikorvausten verovapauteen\Luontoisetuauton vaikutus kilometrikorvausten verovapauteen - vero.fi.html).

Finnish tax practice assumes that the employee should use the company-provided benefit car for the deduction if it is available for private use. However, the deduction rate will be the lower rate of 0.20 euros per kilometer when using the company car.
answer_expected: For commuting between home and the regular workplace (asunnon ja työpaikan väliset matkat) in tax year 2025, the per-kilometer deduction rates depend on the vehicle used: (a) when the employee drives their own car (oma auto), the deduction is 0.27 euro/km; (b) when they drive a benefit car (käyttöetuauto, where the employee pays running costs), the deduction is 0.20 euro/km. (For reference, a free company car (vapaan autoedun auto), where the employer covers all costs, also has a 0.20 euro/km commuting rate.) However, the rate alone does not determine the deduction. Finnish tax practice (Verohallinto) is that the commuting deduction is calculated based on the cheapest mode of transport (halvin kulkuneuvo) — typically public transport — and the deduction may only be made based on a private car (own or benefit car) in specific circumstances where public transport is not a feasible option. So even though the employee owns a private car, the deduction is normally not granted at the 0.27 e/km own-car rate by default; the assumption is that they would use the cheapest available means of transport (public transport at the ticket price), and only when the special conditions for own-car/benefit-car use are met do the 0.27 e/km or 0.20 e/km rates apply respectively to that vehicle's actual usage.

question: A Finnish resident taxpayer receives 80,000 euros in dividends from a listed Finnish company in a single tax year. Under current Finnish rules: (a) what portion, if any, is tax-free; (b) what portion is taxable; and (c) at which capital income tax rate(s) is the taxable portion assessed? (Assume the dividend rules referenced in the KB's osingon verovapaa osa guidance apply.)
answer_generated:
(a) 15% of the dividend is tax-free. This amounts to 12,000 euros (15% of 80,000 euros).

(b) 85% of the dividend is taxable. This amounts to 68,000 euros (85% of 80,000 euros).

(c) The taxable portion is assessed at the capital income tax rate of 30%. 

(PerVL 33 a § 1 momentti)
answer_expected: For dividends received by a natural person resident in Finland from a publicly listed Finnish company (julkisesti noteerattu yhtiö), the rule is fixed and does not depend on share value or income thresholds: 85 percent of the dividend is taxable capital income (veronalaista pääomatuloa) and 15 percent is tax-free (verovapaata tuloa). Applied to the 80,000 euro dividend: (a) the tax-free portion is 15% × 80,000 = 12,000 euros; (b) the taxable portion is 85% × 80,000 = 68,000 euros, treated as capital income; (c) the capital income tax rate is 30% on capital income up to 30,000 euros and 34% on the portion exceeding 30,000 euros. So the 68,000 euros of taxable dividend, assuming this is the taxpayer's only capital income, is taxed as follows: the first 30,000 euros at 30% = 9,000 euros, and the remaining 38,000 euros at 34% = 12,920 euros, for a total tax of 21,920 euros. (If the taxpayer has other capital income, the listed-dividend tax-free / taxable split is the same, but the 30,000-euro bracket boundary applies to total capital income.) Additionally, the listed company (or its custodian) withholds a 25.5% advance withholding (ennakonpidätys) on the gross dividend at payment time.

question: An Austrian company owns 28% of a Finnish company and receives a dividend of 200,000 euros. A separate Austrian company owns 20% of the same Finnish company and receives a dividend of 100,000 euros. Under the Finland-Austria double tax treaty, what withholding tax rate applies to each dividend payment, and what is the net amount each Austrian company actually receives?
answer_generated:
Under the Finland-Austria double tax treaty, the applicable withholding tax rates for dividends depend on the ownership percentage of the recipient company in the dividend-paying company. 

For the Austrian company that owns 28% of the Finnish company:
- The ownership of 28% qualifies as a significant ownership stake, typically falling under the definition of a "suorasijoitusosinko" (qualifying for a lower withholding tax rate).
- According to the treaty, the withholding tax rate for such significant ownership stakes is generally 0–5%. For this example, we will assume the lowest possible rate of 0%.
- Therefore, the net amount received by this Austrian company is 200,000 euros (no withholding tax).

For the Austrian company that owns 20% of the Finnish company:
- The ownership of 20% also qualifies as a significant ownership stake, typically falling under the definition of a "suorasijoitusosinko".
- Similarly, the withholding tax rate for such significant ownership stakes is generally 0–5%. For this example, we will assume the lowest possible rate of 0%.
- Therefore, the net amount received by this Austrian company is 100,000 euros (no withholding tax).

In summary:
- The first Austrian company receives 200,000 euros with a 0% withholding tax.
- The second Austrian company receives 100,000 euros with a 0% withholding tax.

(Source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Rajoitetusti verovelvollisen osingot, korot ja rojaltit\Rajoitetusti verovelvollisen osingot, korot ja rojaltit - vero.fi_1.html)
answer_expected: Under Article 10 of the Finland–Austria double tax treaty (Sopimus Suomen tasavallan ja Itävallan tasavallan välillä, SopS 18/2001, in its synthesised form): paragraph 2 sets a general maximum source-state withholding rate of 10% of the gross dividend; but paragraph 3 provides a complete exemption (0%) when the recipient is a company resident in the other contracting state that directly controls (välittömästi hallitsee) at least 10 percent of the voting power of the dividend-paying company. Applying this to both Austrian shareholders: (a) the Austrian company holding 28% of the Finnish company satisfies the at-least-10% direct ownership condition, so Finland may not levy withholding tax on its 200,000-euro dividend — the rate is 0% and the company receives the full 200,000 euros net (subject to any beneficial-ownership / anti-abuse / MLI principal-purpose-test requirements being met); (b) the separate Austrian company holding 20% likewise satisfies the at-least-10% condition, so its 100,000-euro dividend is also withheld at 0%, and the company receives the full 100,000 euros net. Both companies therefore receive the gross dividend in full (200,000 EUR and 100,000 EUR, respectively) with no Finnish withholding tax, because both holdings exceed the 10% direct voting-power threshold in Article 10(3). The 10% rate from Article 10(2) only applies when the recipient is below the 10% direct-ownership threshold (e.g., portfolio holdings) or does not meet the conditions of paragraph 3.

question: A Finnish employee was born in 1958. They are 67 years old and still in active employment. Are they still required to pay TyEL (statutory pension insurance) contributions, and until what age does the obligation continue for someone born in 1958 versus someone born in 1962?
answer_generated: For a Finnish employee born in 1958, the statutory pension insurance (TyEL) contribution obligation ends at the age of 68. According to the context, the vakuuttamisvelvollisuus (obligation to be insured) ends at 68, 69, or 70 years of age, depending on the year of birth (TyEL 4 §:n 2 momentti). For those born in 1958, the upper age limit is 68. For those born in 1962, the upper age limit is 65. Therefore, the employee born in 1958 is still required to pay TyEL contributions at 67 years old, but the obligation will end once they reach 68 years of age. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Kotitalous työn teettäjänä\Kotitalous työn teettäjänä - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: Yes, a 67-year-old employee born in 1958 is still subject to TyEL pension insurance and the employer remains obligated to insure them and the employee's pension contribution is still withheld from their salary. The Finnish pension system has a tiered upper age limit (vakuuttamisvelvollisuuden yläikäraja) that depends on the worker's birth year:  (a) Persons born in 1957 or earlier: insurance obligation ends at the end of the calendar month in which the worker turns 68. (b) Persons born 1958–1961: insurance obligation ends at the end of the calendar month in which the worker turns 69. (c) Persons born 1962 or later: insurance obligation ends at the end of the calendar month in which the worker turns 70.  So for the employee born in 1958, the TyEL insurance obligation continues until the end of the month in which they turn 69 (i.e., until 2027). For someone born in 1962, the obligation continues until the end of the month in which they turn 70.  The lower bound: the employer's obligation to insure begins at the start of the calendar month following the employee's 17th birthday, and TyEL insurance is required when the employee's monthly earnings reach at least 71.72 euro/month (2026 level, adjusted annually with the wage coefficient). MEL has no earnings minimum.  For 2026 the employee's TyEL contribution withheld in pre-collection (eläkevakuutusmaksu used in calculating ennakonpidätys) is 7.30 % of pay, applied between age 17 and the age-based upper limit described above. The same age cut-offs are used in the Tax Administration's 2026 ennakonpidätys decision (pension contribution stops at 68/69/70 depending on birth year cohort).

question: A food delivery platform worker (the primary account holder, lähettitilin pääkäyttäjä) arranges for a friend to work their shifts on two occasions. The friend has no Finnish tax card. (a) What withholding rate applies to the payment made to the friend? (b) Is the entire payment received by the primary worker (including the amount ultimately paid to the friend) initially reported as the primary worker's income? (c) Can the primary worker deduct what they paid the friend?
answer_generated:
(a) The withholding rate that applies to the payment made to the friend is 60%, as per the Ennakoperintäasetus (1124/1996, EPA) 3 §, since the friend does not have a Finnish tax card. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Ulkomaisen liikkeen- ja ammatinharjoittajan tuloverotus Suomessa\Ulkomaisen liikkeen- ja ammatinharjoittajan tuloverotus Suomessa - vero.fi.html)

(b) Yes, the entire payment received by the primary worker, including the amount ultimately paid to the friend, is initially reported as the primary worker's income (työkorvausta). This is because the primary worker (ruokalähettipalvelun pääkäyttäjä) is responsible for the work and the payment is made to them. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Ruokalähettien ja heidän sijaistensa verotuksesta\Ruokalähettien ja heidän sijaistensa verotuksesta - vero.fi.html)

(c) Yes, the primary worker can deduct what they paid the friend as either the salary and related expenses or the amount paid to the substitute as a work compensation. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Ruokalähettien ja heidän sijaistensa verotuksesta\Ruokalähettien ja heidän sijaistensa verotuksesta - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: Verohallinto's official position (Kannanotto: Ruokalähettien ja heidän sijaistensa verotuksesta) addresses exactly this scenario, where a courier (lähettitilin pääkäyttäjä) lets a substitute (sijainen) work via their account.  (a) Withholding rate on the payment to the friend (the substitute): If the substitute has not presented a tax card (verokorttia), the primary courier — acting as the payer to the substitute — must withhold tax at 60 %. This applies regardless of whether the relationship between courier and substitute is treated as an assignment relationship (toimeksiantosuhde, payment is työkorvaus) or an employment relationship (työsuhde, payment is palkka). In both cases, the substitute's missing tax card triggers the 60 % default rate. Note also that the kotitalous (household) ennakonpidätys exemption under EPL 9 § 3 mom. does not apply to a courier paying their substitute.  (b) Initial reporting of the platform's payment: Yes. The food delivery platform pays the entire fee to the account holder (the primary courier) for all jobs done through the account, even those performed by the substitute. The whole amount is the primary courier's veronalaista ansiotuloa (taxable earned income), normally reported as työkorvaus when the courier uses an invoicing service (laskutuspalveluyritys) that pays them as työkorvaus, or as elinkeinotoiminnan tuloa if the courier operates as an entrepreneur with a business ID. A laskutuspalveluyritys cannot pay the courier wages for work the courier did not personally perform — wages can only be paid to the person who actually did the work and in whose name the work was invoiced.  (c) Deductibility of payments to the substitute: Yes. The primary courier may deduct, as tulonhankkimiskuluja (income-acquisition costs), either (i) the wages plus side costs (sivukulut) paid to the substitute if treated as an employee, or (ii) the työkorvaus paid to the substitute if treated as a self-employed assignment. If the courier requests a tax card, they should report as income their estimate of the gross amount invoiced from the platform (covering both their own and the substitute's work), and report the amount they expect to pay to the substitute as tulonhankkimiskuluja under deductions. Beyond income tax, the courier paying wages must also report the wage and withholding to tulorekisteri and pay the employer's sairausvakuutusmaksu; for työkorvaus paid to a substitute who is not in the ennakkoperintärekisteri, the courier must report the työkorvaus to tulorekisteri.

question: An employee commutes by bicycle 12 km each way to work, 220 days per year. Their neighbour commutes the same route by moped. (a) What is each person's annual commuting deduction under the 2025 rules? (b) Does it matter if the bicycle is a regular bicycle or an e-bike?
answer_generated:
(a) The employee commuting by bicycle can claim a deduction of 100 euros for the year, as this is the maximum amount allowed for bicycles, kevyt sähköajoneuvot, and sähkökäyttöiset jalankulkuavustajat or korvaajat (PerVL 2.3.2, source: Verohallinnon yhtenäistämisohjeet vuodelta 2025 toimitettavaa verotusta varten - vero.fi.html).

The neighbour commuting by moped can claim a deduction of 0.13 €/km. For a 12 km one-way commute, the total annual deduction is calculated as follows:
12 km * 2 (round trip) * 220 days * 0.13 €/km = 686.40 euros (PerVL 2.3.2, source: Verohallinnon yhtenäistämisohjeet vuodelta 2025 toimitettavaa verotusta varten - vero.fi.html).

(b) It does not matter if the bicycle is a regular bicycle or an e-bike. Both types of bicycles fall under the same category for the purpose of the deduction, and the maximum deduction remains 100 euros per year (source: Matkakustannusten vähentäminen palkansaajan verotuksessa - vero.fi.html).
answer_expected: Under the Verohallinto table 'Eri kulkuneuvojen matkakuluvähennys vuonna 2025' (used for the 2025 tax return and as guidance for the 2026 tax card), commuting (asunnon ja työpaikan välinen matka) deductions for these two vehicles are different in nature. Note that the asunnon ja työpaikan välinen matkakuluvähennys is in principle calculated using the cheapest available means of transport; the rates below assume that condition is met (e.g., no public transport available at a comparable cost) and the answers ignore the omavastuu (basic deductible) and annual maximum that the taxpayer would still apply on form 3005.  (a) Annual amounts: - Bicycle commuter (12 km each way × 2 × 220 days = 5,280 km/year):   Bicycles, including e-bikes (sähköpyörä), are deducted as a flat 100 euro per year — distance and number of commuting days do not matter. So the gross matkakuluvähennys is 100 euro/year before omavastuu. After the standard omavastuu, in practice this means no net deduction for most taxpayers, since the omavastuu exceeds 100 euro. - Moped commuter (same 5,280 km/year):   Moped (mopo) is deducted at 0,13 e/km. So 5,280 km × 0,13 e = 686.40 euro/year (gross, before omavastuu).  (b) Regular bicycle vs e-bike: No, it does not matter — Verohallinto's 2025 table explicitly groups 'Polkupyörä (myös sähköpyörä)' in the same row at 100 e/year. The same 100 e/year limit applies regardless of bicycle type. Two caveats from the table footnotes: (1) if the employer provides a verovapaa polkupyöräetu (tax-free bicycle benefit), the employee cannot deduct cycling commuting costs at all; (2) if part of the bicycle benefit is taxable, the employee may deduct an amount equal to that taxable portion, but at most 100 euro/year.

question: A 66-year-old taxpayer has three dependent children aged 8, 12, and 20. Their earned income is 45,000 euros. (a) What is their base work income tax credit (työtulovähennys) percentage and maximum? (b) By how much is the credit increased for the qualifying dependent children? (c) At 45,000 euros income, has the credit begun to phase out, and at what rate?
answer_generated:
(a) The base work income tax credit (työtulovähennys) percentage is 18%, and the maximum amount for a taxpayer who has turned 65 before 2026 is 4,630 euros. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Verotettavan tulon laskeminen henkilöverotuksessa\Verotettavan tulon laskeminen henkilöverotuksessa - vero.fi.html)

(b) The credit is increased by 105 euros for each qualifying dependent child under 17 years old. Since the taxpayer has two children under 17 (aged 8 and 12), the increase is 105 euros x 2 = 210 euros. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Verotettavan tulon laskeminen henkilöverotuksessa\Verotettavan tulon laskeminen henkilöverotuksessa - vero.fi.html)

(c) At 45,000 euros income, the credit has begun to phase out. The phase-out rate is 2% for the portion of the income that exceeds 35,000 euros, up to 50,550 euros. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Verotettavan tulon laskeminen henkilöverotuksessa\Verotettavan tulon laskeminen henkilöverotuksessa - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: Under section 6.1 (Työtulovähennys) of the Verohallinnon päätös ennakonpidätysprosenttien laskentaperusteista palkkatuloa varten vuodelle 2026, the work income tax credit for 2026 is structured as follows:  (a) Base percentage and maximum: The työtulovähennys is 18.0 % of the qualifying earned income (palkka, työkorvaukset, ansiotulo-osinko, jaettavan yritystulon ansiotulo-osuus, etc.). The maximum credit is 3 430 euro.  (b) Increase for dependent children — and for being 65+: - Each alaikäinen lapsi (under-18 child) of whom the taxpayer is huoltaja at year-end increases the maximum by 105 euro. Single huoltaja: the increase is doubled (210 euro) for each child of whom they are sole huoltaja.   In this case the children aged 8 and 12 qualify (they are alaikäisiä). The 20-year-old does NOT qualify because they are no longer alaikäinen. So the child-based increase is 2 × 105 = 210 euro (or 2 × 210 = 420 euro if the taxpayer is a single huoltaja and is the sole huoltaja of those two children). - Because the taxpayer has turned 65 before the start of the tax year (they are 66), the maximum is also raised by 1 200 euro.   So the increased maximum is 3 430 + 210 + 1 200 = 4 840 euro (single sole-huoltaja variant: 3 430 + 420 + 1 200 = 5 050 euro). The 20-year-old child does not contribute to this increase.  (c) Phase-out at 45 000 euro income: Yes, the credit has begun to phase out. The credit is reduced by 2.00 % of puhdas ansiotulo above 35 000 euro, and the reduction continues up to 50 550 euro of puhdas ansiotulo. Indicative reduction at 45 000 euro: (45 000 − 35 000) × 2.00 % = 200 euro reduction (assuming puhdas ansiotulo equals roughly the 45 000 euro figure given). Applied to the increased maximum, the credit at this income level would be approximately 4 840 − 200 = 4 640 euro (or 5 050 − 200 = 4 850 euro for a single sole huoltaja). The work income tax credit is then deducted from state earned-income tax first, and any unused portion is deducted from kunnallisvero, sairausvakuutuksen sairaanhoitomaksu and kirkollisvero in proportion to those taxes.  Note: 'Puhdas ansiotulo' is not exactly the same as gross earned income; in practice it is computed after natural deductions and tulonhankkimisvähennys (750 euro for wage earners). The phase-out figures above use the 45 000 euro number as a rough proxy and could shift by a few euros once the actual puhdas ansiotulo is computed.

question: A Finnish lottery winner intends to transfer 200,000 euros of their winnings to their 10-year-old grandchild. (a) Is this transfer tax-free as a lottery win for the grandchild? (b) What tax category applies instead? (c) Does this exceed the gift tax threshold, and what is the deadline for filing the relevant return?
answer_generated:
(a) No, the transfer is not tax-free as a lottery win for the grandchild. The transfer is considered a gift and is subject to gift tax.

(b) The tax category that applies is gift tax (lahjavero).

(c) Yes, this exceeds the gift tax threshold. According to the 2026 gift tax threshold, the lower limit for gift tax is 7,500 euros. Since the transfer amount is 200,000 euros, it significantly exceeds this threshold. The deadline for filing the gift tax return is typically by the end of the month following the month in which the gift was received. For example, if the gift is received in January, the return must be filed by the end of February. (Source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Ennakkoperintö verotuksessa\Ennakkoperintö verotuksessa - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: (a) No. The transfer is not a tax-free lottery win for the grandchild. Under Finnish lottery rules (arpajaislaki), only the identified player who registered, bought the ticket and is the registered winner can receive a tax-free lottery prize under TVL 85 §. A minor (alaikäinen) is by law (arpajaislaki 14 a §) prohibited from playing money games and cannot register as a Veikkaus player; consequently, transferring a winning (or part of it) to a minor is always treated as a gift, not as a tax-exempt lottery win. (b) The transfer falls under gift taxation (lahjavero) per the Inheritance and Gift Tax Act (Perintö- ja lahjaverolaki, PerVL) 18 §: when ownership of property is transferred as a gift, gift tax is payable. (c) Yes — 200,000 EUR vastly exceeds the gift-tax threshold. From 1.1.2026 the threshold for taxable gifts is 7,500 EUR (gifts received from the same donor within a rolling 3-year period are aggregated against this limit). The donee (i.e., the grandchild — through the legal guardian) must file a gift-tax return (lahjaveroilmoitus) with the Finnish Tax Administration within 3 months of receiving the gift. Failure to file may trigger a tax surcharge (veronkorotus) and, for large amounts, may meet the elements of an aggravated tax fraud offence (törkeä veropetos), which carries up to four years' imprisonment.

question: A company cafeteria has direct meal costs of 7.00 euros per meal. Using the standard indirect cost percentage recognized in Finnish tax practice, what is the full cost per meal used to assess whether the taxable benefit threshold is met? What VAT rate is then applied on that total amount for the meal service?
answer_generated: The full cost per meal, including both direct and indirect costs, is calculated as follows: The direct costs are 7.00 euros, and the standard indirect cost percentage recognized in Finnish tax practice is 22%. Thus, the indirect costs are 22% of 7.00 euros, which equals 1.54 euros. Adding these together, the full cost per meal is 8.54 euros (7.00 euros + 1.54 euros). The VAT rate applied on this total amount for the meal service is 13.5%. (Source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Henkilöstöruokailun arvonlisäverotuksesta\Henkilöstöruokailun arvonlisäverotuksesta - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: Under the Tax Administration's guidance on the VAT treatment of staff meals (Henkilöstöruokailun arvonlisäverotuksesta), where the employer's own indirect costs have not been separately determined, the taxpayer may use 22% of direct costs as a proxy for indirect costs. With direct costs of 7.00 EUR/meal: indirect costs = 22% × 7.00 = 1.54 EUR, so the total (direct + indirect) cost per meal is 8.54 EUR. This 8.54 EUR figure is the basis for assessing the taxable fringe benefit (compared with the meal benefit reference value of 8.80 EUR for 2026; if the cost lies in the 8.80–14.00 EUR band, the fringe benefit value is 8.80 EUR, otherwise the actual cost-incl-VAT applies). The reduced restaurant and catering services VAT rate of 13.5% is applied on that 8.54 EUR base (i.e., own-use VAT = 13.5/100 × 8.54 = approximately 1.15 EUR per meal), as restaurant and catering services are taxed at the reduced 13.5% rate.

question: A foreign specialist arrives in Finland to take up a key personnel position on September 1, 2026. (a) By what date must they or their employer apply for the 32% key personnel withholding tax rate? (b) What rate applies to salary paid before the application is approved? (c) When does the 48-month clock start?
answer_generated:
(a) The application for the 32% key personnel withholding tax rate must be made by December 1, 2026. This is within 90 days from the start of employment on September 1, 2026 (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Avainhenkilöiden verotus\Avainhenkilöiden verotus - vero.fi.html).

(b) Before the application is approved, the standard withholding tax rate applicable to non-key personnel would apply. However, if the conditions for key personnel status are met, the employer can apply the 32% rate even before the application is formally approved, as long as the conditions remain unchanged (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Avainhenkilöltä perittävä lähdevero vuodesta 2020 alkaen\Avainhenkilöltä perittävä lähdevero vuodesta 2020 alkaen - vero.fi.html).

(c) The 48-month clock starts from the beginning of the employment, which is September 1, 2026 (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Kannanotot\Avainhenkilöltä perittävä lähdevero vuodesta 2020 alkaen\Avainhenkilöltä perittävä lähdevero vuodesta 2020 alkaen - vero.fi.html).
answer_expected: (a) Under section 4 of the Foreign Key Personnel Withholding Tax Act (Laki ulkomailta tulevan palkansaajan lähdeverosta, 1551/1995), the application must be filed in writing with the Finnish Tax Administration before the work in Finland begins or, at the latest, within 90 days from the start of the work. For a start date of 1 September 2026, the absolute deadline is therefore 30 November 2026 (90 calendar days from 1 September 2026). (b) Until the application is approved and the key-personnel withholding tax card has been issued, the standard non-resident or resident taxation regime applies to the salary; once approved, the key-personnel rate is applied prospectively to salary paid from the start of the qualifying work period as long as the conditions are met. The cited Verohallinto kannanotto (Avainhenkilöltä perittävä lähdevero vuodesta 2020 alkaen) confirms the rate is 32% (reduced from 35% for salary paid on or after 1.1.2020). (c) The 48-month period (the maximum validity of the key-personnel tax card) starts from the beginning of the work in Finland (työskentelyn alusta lukien) — i.e., from 1 September 2026 — not from the date of application or approval. The card is valid for the work period requested in the application, but in any event no longer than 48 months from the start of the work.

question: Kaisa inherited a farm from her father in January 2016. Inheritance tax was assessed on the estate's total value as declared in the perukirja. In 2023, Kaisa's family lawyer discovers that the perukirja omitted a valid farm loan liability of 80,000 euros — an error that caused the estate to be overvalued and her inheritance tax to be overpaid. (a) Name of the correction procedure and time limit from when inheritance tax obligation arose? (b) Once aware of changed circumstance, within what time must she notify tax authority? (c) What documentation must accompany her notification? (d) Is 2026 still within the available correction window for a 2016 inheritance?
answer_generated:
(a) The correction procedure is called an "oikaisuvaatimus" (rectification claim). The time limit for making this rectification claim is five years from the beginning of the year following the year in which the inheritance tax obligation arose. According to the context, the inheritance tax obligation arose in 2016, so the rectification claim must be made by the end of 2021 (source: 43 §, Laki perintö- ja lahjaverolain muuttamisesta).

(b) Once aware of the changed circumstances, Kaisa must notify the tax authority within 60 days of the day she received information about the error (source: 98 §, Autoverolaki).

(c) The documentation that must accompany her notification includes:
- The deceased's and the heir's personal identification numbers.
- A clarification of the time when the inheritance tax liability began.
- A list of the assets and debts of the estate and their values at the time the unclaimed estate ended.
- A list of assets and debts that must be considered under the marital property law when determining the rights to the inheritable assets.
- Information on any ongoing disputes or similar matters related to the above points.
- If multiple persons have rights to the inherited property, a clarification of their rights according to the marital property law, inheritance law, and any applicable foreign laws, as well as any testamentary provisions (source: Perintö- ja lahjaverotuksen erityisten ilmoittamisvelvollisuuksien täyttäminen - vero.fi).

(d) No, 2026 is not within the available correction window for a 2016 inheritance. The correction window for inheritance tax is five years from the beginning of the year following the year in which the inheritance tax obligation arose, which means the deadline was the end of 2021 (source: 43 §, Laki perintö- ja lahjaverolain muuttamisesta).
answer_expected: (a) Procedure and time limit from inception of tax liability:  The correction procedure is called perusteoikaisu (literally 'basis correction'). It is the re-assessment of inheritance or gift tax provided for in PerVL (Perintö- ja lahjaverolaki) sections 38 and 39 when the basis on which the tax was originally assessed has changed after assessment, or when new assets or debts are discovered (in which case a täydennysperunkirjoitus, supplementary estate inventory, is also relevant). Vero's Kannanotto explains: 'Tällaisesta perintö- ja lahjaverotuksen toimittamisesta uudelleen käytetään nimitystä perusteoikaisu.'  The outer time limit is 10 years from the start of inheritance or gift tax liability ('Perusteoikaisu voidaan tehdä 10 vuoden kuluessa perintö- tai lahjaverovelvollisuuden alkamisesta'). Inheritance tax liability begins at the deceased's date of death; in Kaisa's case, January 2016 (her father's death). The window therefore runs to January 2026.  (b) Time to notify the tax authority once aware of the change:  The statutory notification deadline is 3 months from when the verovelvollinen (taxpayer) became aware of the basis for the perusteoikaisu, but this duty applies primarily to a taxpayer for whom the perusteoikaisu would INCREASE the tax: 'Sen verovelvollisen, jonka perintö- tai lahjaveron määrää perusteoikaisu nostaa, tulee kolmen kuukauden kuluessa perusteoikaisun perusteesta tiedon saatuaan ilmoittaa asiasta Verohallinnolle, jollei joku muu osakas tai lahjansaaja jo ole ilmoittanut asiasta.'  Kaisa's situation is the reverse — discovery of an unrecorded 80,000 EUR liability that DECREASES the estate's net value and her inheritance tax. The 3-month notification rule with sanctions (see PerVL 36a, 36b on veronkorotukset/myöhästymismaksut) is targeted at upward perusteoikaisu. Kaisa nevertheless files within the same perusteoikaisu framework — she submits her notification at any time within the 10-year window in (a). In practice, she should still notify promptly because the perusteoikaisu must be processed before the 10-year outer limit expires.  (c) Documentation required with the notification:  The Verohallinto's decision (VH/6511/00.01.00/2020), section 4 §, prescribes: 'Verovelvollisen on annettava perintö- ja lahjaverolain 38 §:n 3 momentissa tarkoitetuissa tapauksissa ilmoitus perintö- ja lahjaverotuksen perusteiden muuttumisesta antamalla tiedot ja selvitykset Verohallinnolle sähköisesti Verohallinnon tarjoamassa asiointipalvelussa tai vapaamuotoisesti kirjallisena. Ilmoituksessa tulee esittää selvitys siitä, miten perintö- tai lahjaverotuksen perusteet ovat perintö- tai lahjaverotuksen toimittamisen jälkeen muuttuneet. Ilmoitukseen on liitettävä ositussopimus, perinnönjakosopimus, pesänjakajan päätös, yleisen tuomioistuimen päätös, ulosottoviranomaisen päätös, yksityisoikeudellisen riita-asian sovinnon vahvistava sopimus tai muu vastaava asiakirja, johon perintö- tai lahjaveron perusteiden muutos perustuu. Verovelvollisen on allekirjoitettava vapaamuotoisesti kirjallisena annettava ilmoitus.'  For Kaisa, the documentation accompanying her notification must therefore include: - A written explanation (selvitys) of how the inheritance tax basis has changed after the original assessment — i.e. the discovery that the perukirja omitted an 80,000 EUR farm loan that was a valid liability of the estate at the date of death; - The supporting documents proving the existence and amount of the loan as of the date of death (e.g. the original loan agreement, bank statements, lender confirmation, security documents on the farm) — i.e. 'muu vastaava asiakirja, johon perintö- tai lahjaveron perusteiden muutos perustuu'; - If a paper free-form (vapaamuotoinen) notification is used, it must be signed by Kaisa.  Vero recommends submitting via the OmaVero rectification application (oikaisuvaatimuslomake) or via OmaVero with a free-form attachment that explicitly states the document is intended as a PerVL 38 § perusteoikaisu notification — not merely as an oikaisuvaatimus.  (d) Is 2026 still within the correction window?  Yes — partially. Inheritance tax liability arose in January 2016 with her father's death. The 10-year perusteoikaisu window runs until January 2026. So a notification filed up to and including January 2026 is timely; a notification filed in 2026 after that date would be outside the window. Confidence: medium-high — the corpus states the outer limit unambiguously (10 vuoden kuluessa perintöverovelvollisuuden alkamisesta) but does not give a specific January day; the answer assumes the date is the same date in January as her father's death.

question: Liisa is a Finnish citizen, lived/worked in Ireland 5 years. Earns 95,000 euros/yr from Irish employer, rents Helsinki apartment for 14,000 euros/yr. Maintained Finnish permanent address and Finnish tax residency throughout. (a) Under Finland-Ireland double tax treaty, in which country is her employment income primarily taxable? (b) How is her Finnish rental income of 14,000 euros taxed in Finland — which income category, and at what rate(s)? (c) If she has no other capital income in Finland, what is her Finnish tax liability on the rental income alone (calculate using applicable capital income rates)?
answer_generated:
(a) Under the Finland-Ireland double tax treaty, Liisa's employment income is primarily taxable in Ireland. According to the general rule in such treaties, employment income is taxable in the country where the work is performed (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Kansainväliseltä järjestöltä, EU_lta ja valtioiden edustustoilta saadun palkkatulon verotus\Kansainväliseltä järjestöltä, EU_lta ja valtioiden edustustoilta saadun palkkatulon verotus - vero.fi.html).

(b) Her Finnish rental income of 14,000 euros is taxed in Finland under the category of capital income. The applicable tax rate for capital income in Finland is 30% (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Matkakustannusten vähentäminen palkansaajan verotuksessa\Matkakustannusten vähentäminen palkansaajan verotuksessa - vero.fi.html).

(c) If she has no other capital income in Finland, her Finnish tax liability on the rental income alone is calculated as follows:
- Rental income: 14,000 euros
- Tax rate: 30%
- Tax liability: 14,000 euros * 30% = 4,200 euros

Thus, her Finnish tax liability on the rental income alone is 4,200 euros.
answer_expected: (a) Treaty allocation of employment income — Article 16 (Yksityinen palvelus) of the Finland-Ireland double tax treaty:  The applicable treaty is the 1992 Sopimus Suomen Tasavallan hallituksen ja Irlannin hallituksen välillä tulo- ja myyntivoittoveroja koskevan kaksinkertaisen verotuksen välttämiseksi ja veron kiertämisen estämiseksi (SopS 88/1993), as amended by the OECD multilateral BEPS instrument (yleissopimus) signed 7.6.2017 — the synthesised version is in the corpus.  Article 16(1) of the treaty (Yksityinen palvelus) provides: 'Jollei 17, 19, 20 ja 21 artiklan määräyksistä muuta johdu, verotetaan palkasta ja muusta sellaisesta hyvityksestä, jonka sopimusvaltiossa asuva henkilö saa epäitsenäisestä työstä, vain tässä valtiossa, jollei työtä tehdä toisessa sopimusvaltiossa. Jos työ tehdään tässä toisessa valtiossa, voidaan työstä saadusta hyvityksestä verottaa siellä.'  Liisa is a Finnish tax resident (per the facts) but the employment is exercised in Ireland (work is physically performed in Ireland, paid by an Irish employer). Therefore Ireland has the primary taxing right over the employment income under Article 16(1) — 'jos työ tehdään tässä toisessa valtiossa, voidaan työstä saadusta hyvityksestä verottaa siellä' — Ireland (the source state where the work is performed) may tax.  The Article 16(2) 183-day rule does NOT exempt source-country taxation here, because the conditions (≤183 days in 12-month period, employer not resident in source state, no PE bearing the cost) are not all met — Liisa worked in Ireland for years, and the employer is Irish-resident.  Finland, as residence state, also retains the right to tax the employment income (because Liisa is Finnish-resident and Article 16 grants only a 'voidaan verottaa siellä' source-state right, not exclusive). Finland eliminates double taxation under the treaty's relief article (typically credit method for Irish tax against Finnish tax on the same income). Net result: Ireland is the PRIMARY taxing jurisdiction; Finland imposes residual residence-state tax with a foreign tax credit for the Irish tax.  (b) Finnish rental income — category and rates:  Under Finnish domestic law, rental income from real estate (or shares in a housing company giving right to control an apartment) is pääomatulo (capital income) per the vero.fi guidance 'Pääomatulon veroprosentti': 'Pääomatuloa on varallisuuden tuottama tulo, esimerkiksi vuokratulo, omaisuuden luovutuksesta saatu voitto, osinkotulo, tietyt korkotulot, henkivakuutuksen tuotto ja voitto-osuus.'  Finnish capital income is taxed at progressive rates: - 30 % on the portion up to 30,000 EUR; and - 34 % on the portion exceeding 30,000 EUR.  The rental of a Helsinki apartment by a Finnish-resident person is therefore Finnish-source pääomatulo, taxable in Finland under TVL (and also taxable in Finland on residence basis irrespective of source).  Under the Finland-Ireland treaty, immovable-property rental income (kiinteästä omaisuudesta saatu tulo) under Article 7 is taxable in the situs state (Finland) — and a similar source-state taxing right is given by Article 7(4) for shares whose ownership entitles the holder to use the company's immovable property (typical Finnish asunto-osakeyhtiö share). So Finland has the primary taxing right over Liisa's Helsinki rental income under the treaty as well; Ireland, as residence-of-domicile state if she had been resident there, would normally relieve double taxation — but the facts state Liisa retained Finnish tax residency, so Finland is also the residence state and the issue is purely domestic.  (c) Finnish tax on the 14,000 EUR rental income alone:  Assuming the 14,000 EUR is the NET pääomatulo after deductible vuokraustoiminnan menot (e.g. yhtiövastike, repairs, depreciation), and Liisa has no other capital income in Finland for the year:  - The whole 14,000 EUR is below the 30,000 EUR threshold, so the entire amount is taxed at 30 %. - Tax = 14,000 × 30 % = 4,200 EUR.  Her Finnish tax liability on the rental income alone is thus 4,200 euros.  If the question instead refers to gross rent, the actual taxable pääomatulo would be lower (gross rent minus deductible expenses like maintenance charges, repairs, ASOY reserves, mortgage interest on the rental property, and mahdollinen tappioiden vähennys). The 4,200 EUR figure assumes the 14,000 EUR equals the taxable net pääomatulo.

question: Pekka runs sole proprietorship (toiminimi): VAT-taxable IT consulting (8,500 euros/yr) + VAT-exempt educational training (4,000 euros/yr), total 12,500 euros. Registered for VAT three years ago when he exceeded the then-threshold. Uses shared office equipment. (a) Under current vähäisen toiminnan raja rules (updated from 8,500 to 10,000 as of 1 Jan 2016 — note: there have been further changes; cite what the corpus says), does Pekka's total turnover require him to remain VAT-registered? (b) If he deregisters, is he entitled to any VAT refund for the period during which he was registered? (c) If he remains registered, what proportion of input VAT on shared equipment can he deduct, and on what basis?
answer_generated:
(a) According to the current rules, Pekka's total turnover does not require him to remain VAT-registered. The vähäisen toiminnan raja (threshold for small business) is 20,000 euros as of 1.1.2025. Since Pekka's total turnover is 12,500 euros, which is below this threshold, he is not required to be VAT-registered. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Arvonlisäveroton vähäinen toiminta\Arvonlisäveroton vähäinen toiminta - vero.fi.html)

(b) If Pekka deregisters, he is not entitled to a VAT refund for the period during which he was registered. Once a business has voluntarily registered for VAT, it must continue to pay VAT on all taxable sales, even if the turnover subsequently falls below the threshold. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Verohallituksen kannanottoja yritysverokysymyksiin\Verohallituksen kannanottoja yritysverokysymyksiin - vero.fi.html)

(c) If Pekka remains registered, he can deduct input VAT on shared equipment based on the proportion of his VAT-taxable turnover to his total turnover. In Pekka's case, the proportion would be calculated as follows: 8,500 euros (VAT-taxable IT consulting) / 12,500 euros (total turnover) = 68%. Therefore, Pekka can deduct 68% of the input VAT on shared equipment. (source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Arvonlisäveroton vähäinen toiminta\Arvonlisäveroton vähäinen toiminta - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: (a) Vähäisen toiminnan raja and Pekka's mandatory VAT registration:  The Vero kannanotto 'Joukkoistetun henkilökuljetustoiminnan verotus' (kumottu, but its statement of the Arvonlisäverolaki rule still describes the regime) explicitly cites the threshold history: 'Jos vähäisen toiminnan liikevaihdon raja 8.500 euroa (1.1.2016 alkaen 10.000 euroa) tilikaudessa ylittyy, verovelvollisen on ilmoittauduttava arvonlisäverovelvollisten rekisteriin.' That is, the AVL vähäisen toiminnan raja was 8,500 EUR before 1.1.2016 and 10,000 EUR per tilikausi (financial year) from 1.1.2016. (Note: Subsequent statutory changes have further raised the threshold; the corpus shows only the 1.1.2016 step.)  The relevant turnover is total liikevaihto for VAT purposes — but only the part that consists of TAXABLE supplies counts toward the threshold. VAT-exempt supplies (such as Pekka's educational training under AVL 39 §–40 § / koulutuspalvelujen verovapaus) are excluded from the calculation of the vähäisen toiminnan threshold.  Applying this: - Pekka's VAT-TAXABLE turnover = 8,500 EUR (IT consulting) only. - His VAT-EXEMPT turnover = 4,000 EUR (educational training) is excluded from the threshold count. - The combined 12,500 EUR is irrelevant for the test.  Since 8,500 EUR is below the 10,000 EUR threshold, Pekka's taxable turnover does NOT cross the threshold, and he is NOT required to be VAT-registered. He is free to deregister (or voluntarily remain registered, as AVL allows).  (b) VAT refund for the period he was registered if he deregisters:  There is no automatic refund of VAT paid simply because Pekka subsequently deregisters. The corpus does not directly describe a 'refund on deregistration' for the historical period. Confidence: low for a precise rule. The general AVL principle is that the registered taxpayer charged output VAT on past taxable supplies and was entitled to deduct input VAT for the same period; both are settled period-by-period. After deregistration: - He cannot retroactively reverse output VAT he charged customers (those are due to the State). - He cannot retroactively claim more input VAT than he was already entitled to during the registered period; deductions taken stand. - However, AVL's vähäisen liiketoiminnan alarajahuojennus (small-business relief, AVL 149 a §) gives a sliding-scale relief on output VAT for taxpayers whose annual taxable turnover is between the lower threshold and an upper relief ceiling — Pekka can claim this for the years his taxable turnover fell within the relief band. The corpus does not contain the AVL itself and does not describe alarajahuojennus directly; the existence of any specific 'refund on deregistration' beyond the alarajahuojennus is not confirmed by the corpus.  In short: there is no carve-out 'deregistration refund' — deregistration is prospective; for historical periods, his entitlements are limited to whatever input VAT he was entitled to deduct at the time and any alarajahuojennus relief.  (c) Deduction proportion for input VAT on shared office equipment (if he remains registered):  If Pekka remains VAT-registered and the equipment is used for both VAT-taxable activity (IT consulting) and VAT-exempt activity (training), input VAT must be apportioned (jaettava) between the two uses. Under the kannanotto's principle for mixed-use assets/services (which echoes AVL 117 §): 'Tulonhankkimistoimintaan kohdistuvista menoista voidaan tällöin vähentää vain se osuus, joka kohdistuu tulon hankkimiseen.' Although phrased for income tax, the same input-VAT logic applies for AVL: only the proportion attributable to taxable activity is deductible.  The practical apportionment basis is typically a turnover ratio — since the corpus does not give a more refined method for Pekka's facts, the natural and supportable apportionment is: - Taxable use ratio = 8,500 / (8,500 + 4,000) = 8,500 / 12,500 = 68 % (rounded). - Pekka can deduct 68 % of the input VAT on the shared equipment. - The remaining 32 % (attributable to VAT-exempt training) is non-deductible.  If actual usage can be measured more precisely (e.g. hours-of-use, square-metre-of-office, or other reasonable allocation key), a more accurate ratio may be used. The general principle is: only the share that genuinely serves taxable activity is deductible; the share serving exempt activity is not.

question: Finnish parent (Oy Pohjoinen Ab) 100% owns Austrian subsidiary (Österreich GmbH). 2025 dividend of 600,000 euros to Finnish parent. Held 100% for three consecutive years. (a) Under Finland-Austria double tax treaty, at what withholding rate is the dividend taxed in Austria? (b) How much does Finnish parent actually receive after Austrian withholding? (c) Under Finnish participation exemption (osinkoverovapaus) rules applicable to corporate shareholders with significant holdings, is the received dividend taxable again in Finland?
answer_generated:
(a) Under the Finland-Austria double tax treaty, the dividend is subject to a withholding tax of 5% in Austria. (Source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Rajoitetusti verovelvollisen osingot, korot ja rojaltit\Rajoitetusti verovelvollisen osingot, korot ja rojaltit - vero.fi_1.html)

(b) The Finnish parent company (Oy Pohjoinen Ab) will receive 570,000 euros after the Austrian withholding tax. Calculation: 600,000 euros - (600,000 euros * 5%) = 570,000 euros.

(c) Under Finnish participation exemption (osinkoverovapaus) rules applicable to corporate shareholders with significant holdings, the received dividend is generally exempt from taxation in Finland if the holding has been at least 10% for more than one year. Since Oy Pohjoinen Ab holds 100% of Österreich GmbH for three consecutive years, the received dividend is not taxable in Finland. (Source: data\raw\vero\Syventävät vero-ohjeet\Ohjeet\Kansainvälisen kaksinkertaisen verotuksen poistaminen yhteisöjen verotuksessa\Kansainvälisen kaksinkertaisen verotuksen poistaminen yhteisöjen verotuksessa - vero.fi.html)
answer_expected: (a) Under Article 10(3) of the Finland–Austria income- and capital-tax treaty, when the recipient of the dividend is a company resident in the other contracting state that directly controls (välittömästi hallitsee) at least 10% of the voting power (äänimäärä) of the company paying the dividend, the dividend may NOT be taxed in the source state. Oy Pohjoinen Ab owns 100% of Österreich GmbH, so the Austrian withholding tax under the treaty is 0% (the general 10% maximum in Article 10(2) does not apply because the 10(3) direct-holding exception overrides it). (b) Because the treaty rate is 0%, after applying the treaty Oy Pohjoinen Ab receives the full 600,000 e dividend without Austrian withholding. (Austria may initially apply its domestic withholding under Article 28; any amount exceeding the treaty rate is then refunded on application.) (c) Article 23(2)(b) of the same treaty provides: "Osinko, jonka Itävallassa asuva yhtiö maksaa Suomessa asuvalle yhtiölle, vapautetaan Suomen verosta, jos saaja välittömästi hallitsee vähintään 10 prosenttia osingon maksavan yhtiön äänimäärästä." Because Oy Pohjoinen Ab directly controls 100% (≥ 10%) of the voting power of Österreich GmbH, the dividend is exempt from Finnish tax under the treaty itself, in line with the Finnish participation-exemption regime for inter-company dividends from EEA-resident subsidiaries with sufficient holdings. The dividend is therefore not taxable again in Finland.
